segments.title: Choosing when to invest in first and second rates
segments.summary: A campaign decision about first, second, and third rates illustrates how ship quality and timing can shape naval spending choices.
segments.body: While surveying the game campaign, Dr Clarke notes that his local fleet lacks second rates while Spain has them. He then explains his preference for waiting to build first rates rather than spending on second rates whose advantage is short-lived. The moment is a useful prompt for considering how a navy weighs immediate strength against the value of waiting for a more capable capital ship.

evidence.note: He compares the Spanish second rates with the ships available to his own regional fleet.
He explains his preference for first and third rates over a short-lived investment in second rates.

segments.title: Why a blockade should not close every British trade route
segments.summary: A complaint about the game's blockade mechanic becomes a compact lesson in Britain's resilience as a multi-port trading state.
segments.body: Dr Clarke objects to a game rule that lets one blockaded port collapse the entire economy. He argues that trade would instead move to other ports, especially in Britain's unusually dense port network. The watch point highlights why blockade is a problem of controlling a system of harbours, commerce, and alternative routes rather than merely stopping one harbour.

evidence.note: He contrasts the game's one-port economic collapse with the ability of trade to use Britain's other ports.

segments.title: Matching artillery, infantry, and cavalry to the threat
segments.summary: A short tactical explanation sets out the counterweights between artillery, cavalry, and massed infantry.
segments.body: Dr Clarke frames artillery as the answer to mass infantry, while infantry are needed to resist a cavalry-heavy force. He also distinguishes the cavalry used to threaten artillery from the artillery used against infantry. Although expressed through the game, the moment gives viewers a clear combined-arms vocabulary for reading battlefield decisions.

evidence.note: He describes the roles of artillery, cavalry, and infantry against different opposing force mixes.

segments.title: Using obstacles to funnel an attacking force
segments.summary: A defensive setup uses an outer obstacle belt to channel attackers and an inner line to protect the guns and reserves.
segments.body: Dr Clarke says he is arranging the position as a siege camp, then explains why he pulls troops back from the outer defences. Attackers climbing those obstacles become more exposed to artillery, while the inner line supplies the real protection. The arrangement also releases much of the infantry as a mobile reserve, showing how fieldworks can shape an enemy's approach instead of simply providing cover.

evidence.note: He compares the defensive layout to a siege camp.
He explains how outer defences funnel attackers and inner defences protect the force and preserve manoeuvre infantry.

segments.title: Concentration, terrain, and the vulnerable extended line
segments.summary: A battle plan links the advice against uphill attacks to the danger of an enemy force stretched into a long, exposed line.
segments.body: Dr Clarke starts with the familiar warning not to attack uphill when another option exists, then adds the maxim to march divided and fight concentrated. When the opposing line spreads out, he identifies the gaps and argues that cavalry can turn the exposed flank into a cascade of panic. It is a useful tactical watch point on how terrain, formation, and local concentration can outweigh raw numbers.

evidence.note: He states the uphill-attack warning and the principle of marching divided but fighting concentrated.
He examines the extended line and explains how cavalry could exploit its weak end and gaps.

segments.title: Making an attacker cross the ground first
segments.summary: Dr Clarke explains why he waits for the attacking force: fatigue can matter more than the morale gain of early aggression.
segments.body: Facing an enemy advance, Dr Clarke keeps his own line in place so the attackers must cross the ground and arrive tired. He acknowledges that aggression can bring a morale benefit, but treats fatigue as the more important early-battle constraint. A comparison with fencing makes the larger point clear: patient defence can force the opponent to commit first and begin the fight at a disadvantage.

evidence.note: He explains the fatigue and morale trade-off in waiting for an enemy advance, using fencing as an analogy.
